answer:
**The work is *Old English Elegies* (unknown author), Chapter 4 ("Leodum is minum swylce him mon lac gife;").**[^1]

This chapter is the Old English poem known as *Wulf and Eadwacer*, a brief, riddle-like first-person lament matching your description in full: the speaker describes her people treating her beloved (Wulf) like a prize they would "devour ... should he dare to come in a raiding troop," states "Our fates are not the same," notes Wulf is on one island while she is on another "fast" island "by fen surrounded," and then pivots to address a second man (Eadwacer) about their child.[^2][^3]

The requested quote addressing the named second man (*Eadwacer*) is:  
"Do you hear, Eadwacer? Our wretched whelp / a wolf bears to the woods."[^4]

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, Ch. 4, [chapter](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/04-leodum-is-minum-swylce-him-mon-lac-gife/translation/).
[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, Ch. 4, [¶](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/04-leodum-is-minum-swylce-him-mon-lac-gife/translation/#p-1fe43f).
[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, Ch. 4, [¶](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/04-leodum-is-minum-swylce-him-mon-lac-gife/translation/#p-75b6cd).
[^4]: *Old English Elegies*, Ch. 4, [¶](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/04-leodum-is-minum-swylce-him-mon-lac-gife/translation/#p-7cd654).
